jokes.text: Three, two, one. Happy new year! Of school!
jokes.setup: Standard New Year's countdown building anticipation
jokes.punchline: Subverts expectation - it's celebrating school starting, not New Year's Eve
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection with efficient setup/payoff. Homer's parental joy at dumping kids back at school is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: You're the government's problem now!
jokes.setup: Kids complaining about starting school
jokes.punchline: Homer gleefully passes responsibility to the state
jokes.explanation: Pure Homer - irresponsible parent celebrating dumping his duties. Highly quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: Free at last. Free at last.
jokes.setup: Homer celebrating kids going to school
jokes.punchline: Appropriates MLK's famous civil rights speech for parental relief
jokes.explanation: Bold reference that works due to Homer's obliviousness to the inappropriateness, though borders on cheap cultural reference.

jokes.text: I never learned anything at that suckshack. Who taught you that language? Kid at school. So you did learn something.
jokes.setup: Bart claims he never learned anything at school while using crude language
jokes.punchline: Marge points out the contradiction - he learned bad language there
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic reversal with solid structure. Marge's logical trap for Bart is well-constructed and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Insurance is the greatest deal ever. If I get hurt, I get paid. And man, do I get hurt.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining insurance as a great deal
jokes.punchline: His enthusiasm based on getting hurt frequently
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - turning his clumsiness into a business model. High character integration with his established trait of constant accidents.

jokes.text: Where my Dils at?
jokes.setup: Reading groups have been renamed from flowers to barnyard animals, except Daffodils
jokes.punchline: Teacher uses hip-hop slang to rally his flower-named reading group
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay combining 'Daffodils' with street slang. Economical but not particularly inspired.

jokes.text: I never tire of that.
jokes.setup: Teacher's hip-hop rallying cry for flower reading group
jokes.punchline: Skinner's dry appreciation for the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner - finding mild amusement in bureaucratic absurdity. Understated but character-perfect.

jokes.text: He stinks! We're assessing you, not him. Withdrawn.
jokes.setup: Student objects to Vice-President's Assessment Test
jokes.punchline: Immediately backs down when corrected about what's being assessed
jokes.explanation: Clean misunderstanding joke with quick reversal. Efficient structure though not groundbreaking.

jokes.text: No Child Left Alone act
jokes.setup: Explaining federal education policy
jokes.punchline: Malapropism of 'No Child Left Behind' that accidentally reveals darker truth
jokes.explanation: Clever malapropism that works on multiple levels - sounds similar but implies abandonment rather than inclusion.

jokes.text: Cut the horse bull Seymour!
jokes.setup: Skinner giving flowery explanation of standardized testing
jokes.punchline: Chalmers' attempt to say 'horseshit' while maintaining propriety
jokes.explanation: Perfect Chalmers - wanting to swear but maintaining bureaucratic decorum. Character-specific verbal tic.

jokes.text: Your scores on this test will determine how much money this suckshack gets
jokes.setup: Explaining importance of standardized test
jokes.punchline: Calls the school a 'suckshack' while demanding high scores for funding
jokes.explanation: Good irony - administrator disparaging his own school while demanding performance. Consistent with Chalmers' cynicism.

jokes.text: Accaca-Dabacca-Accacaca
jokes.setup: Teacher explaining test-taking strategy
jokes.punchline: Reducing education to meaningless pattern memorization
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of teaching to the test - education reduced to gibberish patterns. Original and memorable indictment of standardized testing.

jokes.text: Biggest city in Montana? Helena? Wrong!
jokes.setup: Geography question with reasonable answer
jokes.punchline: Teacher rejects correct answer to force pattern adherence
jokes.explanation: Continues the absurdist teaching satire - facts don't matter, only patterns. Good follow-up to the previous pattern joke.

jokes.text: You can't teach this way! Yes, I can! Pretty sure this works! Where are these studies conducted? Ball State.
jokes.setup: Student objects to nonsensical teaching method
jokes.punchline: Teacher's increasingly desperate defense ending with questionable source
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure with teacher getting more defensive. Ball State as dubious research source is nice touch.

jokes.text: What kind of pencil do we take again? Number two, take a number two. Looks like you took a big number two.
jokes.setup: Student asking about pencil type for test
jokes.punchline: Teacher makes toilet humor from 'number two' pencil
jokes.explanation: Obvious toilet humor pun. Lazy writing relying on scatological wordplay without cleverness.

jokes.text: Yes, quite so. As you can see, I'm holding a big number two in my hands, enjoying the weight and feel of it.
jokes.setup: Previous toilet humor about number two pencils
jokes.punchline: Teacher continues the double entendre obliviously
jokes.explanation: Continues the weak toilet humor premise. Slightly better due to teacher's obliviousness creating cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Now I can show-off on the federal level!
jokes.setup: Test day excitement
jokes.punchline: Lisa's academic ambition extends to federal bureaucracy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa - her overachievement extends to government testing. Excellent character integration with economical delivery.

jokes.text: Slurt my snot. I wrote 'slurt my snot' in the ovals on the answer sheet. True story.
jokes.setup: Asked about practice test answers
jokes.punchline: Bart's nonsensical rebellion, proudly documented
jokes.explanation: Pure Bart rebellion - creative nonsense phrase shows his anti-authority creativity. 'True story' adds perfect deadpan emphasis.

jokes.text: Oh no, now who will sell oranges on the off-ramp?
jokes.setup: Bart throwing away his future
jokes.punchline: Homer's specific low expectation for Bart's career
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with specific, vivid image of Bart's 'promising' future

jokes.text: Oh no, now who will sell oranges on the off-ramp?
jokes.setup: Told Bart is throwing away his future
jokes.punchline: Homer's limited career expectations for his son
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - his low expectations perfectly delivered. Dark humor about economic prospects with perfect character voice.

jokes.text: Great jokes make me hungry. I wonder what's in the pantry.
jokes.setup: Family discussion about test scores
jokes.punchline: Homer's random association between humor and hunger
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer non-sequitur. Perfectly in character but not particularly clever or memorable.

jokes.text: I warned you. You got a perfect score.
jokes.setup: Ominous warning about test results
jokes.punchline: The 'bad news' is actually perfect performance
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - frames success as punishment. Efficient reversal that sets up the plot twist effectively.

jokes.text: Several? That's more than a few, and almost a bunch!
jokes.setup: Lisa gets 96% on practice test
jokes.punchline: Her mathematical precision applied to vague quantities
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa - applying academic rigor to casual language. Excellent character integration showing her analytical nature.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. This ain't no genius-copter. This is con-air!
jokes.setup: Kids loaded onto what they thought was a reward helicopter
jokes.punchline: Bart realizes it's a prison transport reference
jokes.explanation: Good movie reference that works in context. 'Genius-copter' is a nice made-up word, and the prison transport twist is effective.

jokes.text: Brilliant plan to remove all the underperformers for test day, sir. Yes. All of them.
jokes.setup: Skinner praising the plan to remove bad students
jokes.punchline: Chalmers' emphasis reveals Skinner is also considered an underperformer
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy - Skinner unknowingly praising his own removal. Perfect character dynamics and efficient delivery of the twist.

jokes.text: Can't believe it. Superintendent Chalmers betrayed me. After all the wrapping paper I bought from his daughter.
jokes.setup: Skinner realizing he's been tricked
jokes.punchline: His evidence of loyalty is buying school fundraising wrapping paper
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner - his pathetic measure of professional relationship through school fundraising. Excellent character-specific humor.

jokes.text: We're hiding you in capital city for the day so your numbskullary won't drag down the test scores of the children who are our future.
jokes.setup: Willie explaining the real plan
jokes.punchline: 'Numbskullary' as invented word plus ironic 'children are our future' reference
jokes.explanation: Good invented word 'numbskullary' plus ironic use of inspirational education cliché. Dark humor about educational triage.

jokes.text: Will there be other numbskulls there? Sort of a numbskull Olympics as it were? Let's all act stupid!
jokes.setup: Learning they're being hidden for being dumb
jokes.punchline: Student enthusiastically embraces stupidity as competition
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the premise - turning shame into competitive enthusiasm. 'Numbskull Olympics' is a nice phrase.

jokes.text: This was due two weeks ago! I'm uninsured!
jokes.setup: Homer discovers his insurance payment is overdue
jokes.punchline: Panic at being financially responsible
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier insurance enthusiasm. Homer's terror at responsibility is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: For the first time in my life, I'm financially responsible for my own actions!
jokes.setup: Homer's insurance has lapsed
jokes.punchline: First time being responsible for consequences at his age
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer line - admits he's never been responsible for consequences. Perfect character summation in one economical line.

jokes.text: I'm not in good hands. I'm in no hands! Like a bad neighbor, no one is there!
jokes.setup: Homer panicking about lack of insurance
jokes.punchline: Parodies insurance slogans to emphasize his vulnerability
jokes.explanation: Clever inversion of famous insurance slogans. Good structure building from Allstate to State Farm references.

jokes.text: Imagine each question is a baby chick. If you answer wrong, the chick dies. Shut up, they would've died anyway.
jokes.setup: Skinner's disturbing test anxiety advice involving baby chicks
jokes.punchline: Student's callous dismissal of hypothetical chick deaths
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark humor - Skinner's inappropriate motivation technique met with student's casual nihilism. Unexpected and memorable.

jokes.text: What? They're all equally valid. That can't be! In life, everything only has one answer!
jokes.setup: Lisa encounters ambiguous test question
jokes.punchline: Her naive belief in absolute answers conflicts with reality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa crisis - her worldview challenged by test ambiguity. Excellent character development moment disguised as comedy.

jokes.text: Piece of cake, huh? Ah, I'm writing on my shirt!
jokes.setup: Student's overconfidence about the test
jokes.punchline: Immediate failure at basic task of writing on paper
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy with good ironic reversal. Efficient but not particularly original.

jokes.text: You're stuck babysitting us losers, which makes you the real loser.
jokes.setup: Bart talking to Principal Skinner on the bus
jokes.punchline: Logical trap making Skinner the biggest loser
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart logic trap for authority figure. Good structure and perfect character voice - Bart finding the angle to win.

jokes.text: I am a successful principal who paints houses in the summertime. You painted our garage and it was peeling by October! Your dad insisted I use an inferior primer.
jokes.setup: Skinner defending himself from loser accusation
jokes.punchline: Reveals embarrassing side job and failure at it, then deflects blame
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner - reveals humiliating details while trying to defend himself. Excellent character-specific desperation and blame deflection.

jokes.text: Sorry. I was mesmerized by the little boy's dance.
jokes.setup: Otto not pulling over when child needs bathroom
jokes.punchline: Admits to being distracted by child's desperation dance
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe humor with Otto's characteristic obliviousness. Visual gag we can infer but not particularly strong.

jokes.text: I finished before we came in.
jokes.setup: Asked if he's finished in the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Ralph reveals he had an accident before entering
jokes.explanation: Classic Ralph non-sequitur revealing uncomfortable truth. Perfect character voice but relies on bathroom humor.

jokes.text: Oh, God. We're at the corner of Cesar Chavez Way and Martin Luther King Boulevard. ¡Ay, caramba!
jokes.setup: Bus breaks down in unfamiliar neighborhood
jokes.punchline: Street names indicate they're in a rough area, confirmed by Bart's reaction
jokes.explanation: Observational humor about urban geography stereotypes. Efficient setup but borders on cheap stereotype humor.

jokes.text: You mean back to the poor house. Fine, back.
jokes.setup: Homer threatens they'll go to the poor house
jokes.punchline: Marge corrects that they'd be returning there
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Marge's realistic assessment of their financial status. Efficient correction with perfect timing.

jokes.text: You mean back to the poor house. Fine, back.
jokes.setup: Homer's dramatic 'poor house' threat
jokes.punchline: Marge's correction revealing they're already poor
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy with Marge deflating Homer's drama and Homer's quick acceptance

jokes.text: Book club? That's the most stupid boring thing ever! Oh, thank you.
jokes.setup: Marge mentions her book club
jokes.punchline: Homer insults it then realizes he needs the distraction
jokes.explanation: Good character reversal - Homer's desperation overriding his preferences. Quick misdirection works well.

jokes.text: I've been cleaved!
jokes.setup: Accident during book club meeting
jokes.punchline: Dramatic word choice for getting cut by kitchen knife
jokes.explanation: Good word choice - 'cleaved' is more dramatic than 'cut.' Efficient physical comedy setup.

jokes.text: Lisa, like Captain Kirk, I'm not supposed to interfere. But like TJ Hooker, I say what's on my mind.
jokes.setup: Skinner wants to help Lisa with the test
jokes.punchline: References two William Shatner characters to justify contradictory actions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Skinner moment - using obscure TV references to justify breaking rules. Perfect character voice and unexpected reference pairing.

jokes.text: Get down on your knees, pray to your God, and ask him-- no demand he tell you the answer. And if he won't, he is no God of yours!
jokes.setup: Skinner giving increasingly desperate test advice
jokes.punchline: Escalates to theological ultimatum over test answers
jokes.explanation: Exceptional escalation to absurdist extreme - demanding God provide test answers or face rejection. Perfect structure building to theological crisis.

jokes.text: Sure thing, Such.
jokes.setup: Skinner demands to be called 'Principal Skinner' and 'you will address me as such'
jokes.punchline: Student literally calls him 'Such' as requested
jokes.explanation: Perfect wordplay trap - student takes Skinner literally. Excellent economy and timing in the reversal.

jokes.text: Why do terrible things always happen to wonderful people at 3:01 P.M. Which, for the record, is the correct time.
jokes.setup: Burns witnessing Homer's injury right after insurance lapse
jokes.punchline: His narcissistic focus on being right about the time during tragedy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns - completely missing the point while being pedantically correct. Excellent character-specific narcissism.

jokes.text: At this point in a marriage, a wife should know what her husband can do and what he can't. Who was I to think you can mail an envelope?
jokes.setup: Homer asking if Marge is mad about the insurance payment
jokes.punchline: Marge's resigned acceptance of Homer's incompetence at basic tasks
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Marge moment - realistic marital resignation delivered with perfect timing. Mailing an envelope as insurmountable task is perfectly Homer.

jokes.text: Baby, I hope you've got snuggle insurance... 'cos I'm about to file a claim.
jokes.setup: Homer's insurance crisis throughout episode
jokes.punchline: Turns insurance terminology into romantic come-on
jokes.explanation: Good callback to insurance theme with romantic wordplay. Decent Homer pickup line but not exceptional.

jokes.text: You like all boys? Shut up! A child is in danger under my aegis!
jokes.setup: Skinner says he was once a boy and like all boys...
jokes.punchline: Student mishears as sexual innuendo, Skinner panics and uses fancy word 'aegis'
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with Skinner's typical overreaction. 'Aegis' is perfect Skinner vocabulary choice under pressure.

jokes.text: I'm using the principal of conservation of angular momentum to turn the container!
jokes.setup: Students say Skinner is 'spazzing out' during action sequence
jokes.punchline: Skinner describes his heroics in precise scientific terms
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner - turning action hero moment into physics lesson. Excellent character consistency even during crisis.

jokes.text: That's the kind of stuff they should teach in school! We do teach it in school. You're too busy eating sugar snacks and horsing around!
jokes.setup: Student impressed by Skinner's physics explanation
jokes.punchline: Skinner reveals they already teach this, student doesn't pay attention
jokes.explanation: Good educational satire - students don't realize they're already learning what they want to learn. Classic ironic reversal.

jokes.text: Learning can teach you things. Education rules.
jokes.setup: Student has revelation about education value
jokes.punchline: Expresses profound realization in simplistic terms
jokes.explanation: Mild humor in the obviousness of the statement. Economical but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: By seeing their principal run around on top of a shipping container.
jokes.setup: Skinner reflecting on educational philosophy
jokes.punchline: His realization that his absurd heroics were more educational than testing
jokes.explanation: Good character growth moment with educational satire

jokes.text: Drop that number two. Yes, I know what that means.
jokes.setup: Skinner telling kids to put down pencils
jokes.punchline: Acknowledges the earlier toilet humor callback
jokes.explanation: Meta acknowledgment of earlier weak joke. Self-aware but doesn't elevate the original premise.

jokes.text: Hurray! I'm a brainy outcast again!
jokes.setup: Test being thrown out means Lisa's back to normal status
jokes.punchline: Lisa celebrates returning to social exile
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa - celebrating her return to intellectual isolation. Great character consistency and economical delivery.
